Title: Transformer Capacity Contribution / Authorization Game – Farmers decide whether to contribute to authorized transformer capacity or not. Some farmers already contributed; others may free-ride. This is a public goods game. The text: "When one farmer pays for authorization or capacity improvement, other connected farmers can still benefit from improved voltage quality. This creates a free-rider incentive for non-contributors and makes contributors bear disproportionate private costs." So it's a public goods dilemma. Could be modeled as a 2-player game: Contribute or Not. Mutual contribute yields improved reliability (benefit) minus cost; free-ride yields benefit without cost; mutual not contribute yields low reliability. Payoffs: free-ride (others contribute) is best (3); mutual contribute is second (2); mutual not contribute is third (1); contribute alone is worst (0) because you pay cost but no benefit if others don't contribute? Actually, if only one contributes, does capacity improve? The text says capacity upgrades improve reliability for the group, but if only one pays, the capacity might be insufficient? It says "When one farmer pays for authorization or capacity improvement, other connected farmers can still benefit from improved voltage quality." So even unilateral contribution can provide some benefit? But then free-riding is still better. The dilemma: if too many avoid contributing, transformer remains overloaded. So if only one contributes, maybe the improvement is small but still some benefit? However, the text also suggests that capacity improvement requires enough contributors. I'd interpret as: if no one contributes, reliability stays low; if some contribute, reliability improves for all, but contributors bear cost. So unilateral contribution yields some benefit to all, but cost only to contributor. So contributor's net payoff might be lower than non-contributor. So ordinal: best is not contribute while others do (3); next best is contribute while others do (2) (you get benefit but pay cost); next is not contribute while others don't (1) (low reliability but no cost); worst is contribute while others don't (0) (you pay cost, little or no benefit). That's a typical public goods game (prisoner's dilemma if 2 players). But with 2 players, if both contribute, both get 2; both defect, both get 1; one contributes, one defects: defector gets 3, contributor gets 0. That's a PD. So this is a distinct game.
